data.title: BillionToOne: Delivering And Lagging At The Same Time
data.description:
I have been covering shares of BillionToOne, Inc. (BLLN) since its public offering, as its vision, its products, and its operating momentum have triggered my real interest from the outset.

Its cutting-edge molecular diagnostics for prenatal and oncology testing has been driving not just impressive growth but actually allowed the business to become profitable as well. Strategic agreements and positive research results boosted momentum on top of this, although the business remains largely a one-trick pony.

Having ended the year on a strong note, the company calls for more growth in 2026. This outlook feels conservative, which, in combination with the setback in the share price, makes me willing to increase my exposure here.

Ending 2025 On A Solid Note

Early in March, BillionToOne reported a 113% increase in fourth-quarter sales to $96.1 million. This momentum is driven by the prenatal clinical testing business, growing sales some 99% to $86.1 million. This is largely complemented by a $9.1 million revenue contribution from oncology clinical testing, a number increasing by a factor of 8 times on the year before.

Growth is driven by a combination of pricing and the number of tests performed, the latter increasing some 47% on the year before, as average selling prices have been strong as well.

Digging into the results, the oncology sales growth has been a bit soft, as a $9.1 million revenue number in the fourth quarter, even while increasing by a factor of 8 times year-over-year, was up just about 5% on a sequential basis. It should be said that oncology sales have been lumpier in the past and that overall sales growth came in a bit stronger than expected.

Strong growth and high gross margins made it so that quarterly GAAP operating profits just surpassed $10 million, for low double-digit margins. The company reported full-year operating profits of $16 million on $305 million in sales.

Having previously already guided for 2026 sales to come in between $415 and $430 million, this guidance was hiked by $430 to $445 million. On average that works out to around $110 million per quarter, suggesting continuous sequential improvements, although it still feels as if the guidance might be conservative.

Moreover, the company launched an expanded Red Blood Cell Fetal Antigen (NIPT) offering as well as a Platelet Fetal Antigen (NIPT) offering. Besides, the Northstar PGx and Select CH tests were launched for oncology products, although no individual expectations have been provided for these products, although they might help to diversify away from the prenatal test revenues.

About Expectations

Trading down to $75 per share following the release of the results, the 39.4 million shares (on a diluted basis) represent a $2.95 billion equity valuation. This includes about $440 million in net cash holdings; as adjusted for that, operating assets are valued at around $2.5 billion.

With revenues seen around $437.5 million this year, forward sales multiples are seen at just 5.7 times sales. This follows >40% sales growth anticipated this year, as that feels potentially conservative.

Do Investors Have The Fear Of The Unknown?

The mission of BillionToOne was to remove the fear of the unknown with technologies designed to push beyond limitations in diagnostics, with the ultimate goal of being able to detect diseases with just a single molecule.

The business has been developed on the basic question of what is the most challenging and most impactful global healthcare problem. BillionToOne aims to create actionable insights from single blood draws, which not just provide actionable results but also offer personalized recommendations as well across a range of indications.

Following the IPO in November of last year, shares were priced at $60 per share but jumped to $120 on the first day of trading, now having given up much of the initial gains, trading at levels in the mid-seventies. Part of this comes as the company sees growth slowing down, as triple-digit growth rates have come down to double-digit percentages.

What Is Next?

In between a solid fourth quarter and a solid 2026 guidance (being hiked by fifteen million on the year before), I believe that the company is actually guiding conservatively, all while expectations come down further. Moreover, the company reached an important deal with Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) towards year-end 2025 in hemolytic diseases of fetuses and newborns, while some positive research results have been announced as well.

At that point, that was enough for me to hold a modest long position towards year-end, as I was looking to learn more about the 2026 performance. Looking into the year, I conclude that the fourth quarter results were some kind of mixed bag. While the headline numbers for the fourth quarter looked good, pricing was a big part of this, with sequential volume growth from the third quarter up just 3%, and the oncology franchise saw little sequential growth.

However, the conference call provided comfort to me as the outlook for the year looks somewhat deliberately soft, as the company calls out the fact that the guidance does not really factor in a contribution from recent launches. Moreover, the company touts a coverage deal with United Healthcare (UNH), which does not necessarily increase the number of covered lives but really reduces friction to access between patients and caregivers.

All this makes me feel comfortable in the sense that the guidance is conservative, and with profitability now achieved, earnings multiples remain high, but the real operational leverage potential is there. This makes me continue to be upbeat, as I am dipping my toes a bit further into the water, adding to a small speculative position.

This follows a substantial re-rating in the sales multiples, the operating leverage displayed, and the expectation that growth will surprise on the upside.

Similar high-growth stories are covered at Value In Corporate Events.

data.title: Surging Oil Prices May Be About To Jolt Markets
data.description:
If one thing seems clear, it is that oil, rates, and the dollar are all not only heading higher but have probably only just started their ascent. If this continues, the implications for the stock market could be significant as financial conditions tighten and dollar funding costs rise.

The conflict in Iran has awakened the sleepy oil market that has failed to participate in the commodity boom seen in precious metals. Should oil prices (CL1:COM) (CO1:COM) continue to rise, it would send ripple effects across the market, which could be substantial. Since 2022, interest rates have been highly correlated with oil prices, suggesting rates are likely to follow oil prices higher. A stronger dollar (DXY) is likely to follow.

Economic Data Moves To The Side

Even though the February job report was an epic disaster, showing a decline of 92,000 jobs, the 10-year rate (US10Y) is still rising by 3 bps as of 10 AM ET on March 6. That is because the 10-year rate is more closely tied to oil prices, since the market knows that much of the moderation in inflation over the past four years is due to a sharp decline in energy prices, suggesting the market now sees oil's disinflationary effects as largely over.

Oil has not only seen a solid rise in its price, but WTI Crude has broken out above $88, and technically, a push higher could lead to a further breakout that might send oil prices well into the mid-$90s. Additionally, the chart shows that a golden cross has formed, which is when the 50-day moving average rises above the 200-day moving average; this typically serves as a bullish indicator.

That said, oil has seen its relative strength index rise above 70 and the price rise above the upper Bollinger Band, which together can be a sign of overbought conditions. We have seen this overbought condition in precious metals several times, and in those cases, the metal often consolidated sideways for some time before making another move higher.

Rates And The Dollar At Risk

While risks to oil going higher are real, the risk to rates going even higher is even greater. Rates, by comparison to the move in oil, have not moved nearly as much, and that is probably because the market is trying to determine whether oil prices are making a sustainable or unsustainable move higher.

But what is also clear is that 10-year rates, and rates more broadly, have moved very closely with the price of oil since 2022, and if oil prices continue to rise or even just flatten out, it is likely to drag rates higher as well, even as risks of stagflation or an economic slowdown remain on the horizon.

This is very much true for the dollar index as well, and so what we are seeing right now in the market is a rare combination of the oil, rates, and the dollar all rising at the same time. The danger, of course, is if they continue to rise or stay elevated for some time, because together they will work to tighten financial conditions and lead to an economic slowdown as that happens.

The Spillover Into Stocks

This can be problematic for stocks, too, because as the dollar gains strength, the demand for dollar funding costs is likely to increase, and cross-currency basis swaps are likely to become more negative, making it more expensive to hedge the U.S. dollar. That is already happening with the USDJPY (USD:JPY) 5-year cross-currency basis swap, and historically, the stock market has followed this spread very closely, so as the basis becomes more negative, it will put more pressure on equities. On top of that, the tightening of financial conditions does not play well for equity markets either.

At this point, oil is in the driver's seat, and the longer oil stays elevated, the more pressure it will place on markets and the US economy.

data.title: BitFuFu: Attractive As Bitcoin Nears A Potential Inflection Point
data.description:
Being a crypto investor has not been easy in the past year or so. After seeing Bitcoin hit an all-time high north of $120,000, investors have had to stomach a staggering decline in BTC prices. At one point earlier last month, the drawdown from all-time highs eclipsed 50%. What is even more concerning is that this halving of BTC prices occurred within just four months. Although I do not have direct exposure to BTC, as I explained in a recent analysis on Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN), the crypto winter has affected my portfolio indirectly because of my exposure to a few crypto stocks.

This brings me to BitFuFu, Inc. (FUFU). Although I do not own BitFuFu stock, I have covered FUFU a few times over the past 12 months as I found the company deeply undervalued relative to its peers based on a market cap/hashrate comparison. Today, amid interesting macro developments, I believe FUFU offers a unique value proposition to long-term investors wanting to gain exposure to BTC.

Global Money Supply Growth Trends Paint A Promising Picture For Bitcoin

Bitcoin has made a strong comeback since last Saturday. As of this writing, BTC is up 12% since the start of Middle East tensions and is trading just below $71,500. This trend reversal comes on the back of investors fleeing risk assets to seek the safety of assets with perceived hedging benefits such as gold and Bitcoin. Given the macro risks we are facing today involve security risks, Bitcoin is actually in a unique position to emerge as the hedging instrument of choice among both retail and institutional investors as it hedges against adverse geopolitical developments as well as sovereign risk. This is because BTC is decentralized unlike any fiat currency.

On the other hand, if inflation spikes as a result of persistently high oil prices, the Fed is likely to delay its rate cuts. This could be bad news for cryptos including Bitcoin. Empirical evidence suggests cryptos perform well when the global money supply increases. The below chart illustrates this correlation between global money supply (M2) and BTC prices.

Exhibit 1: Global M2 supply and BTC prices

This is where things get interesting.

Despite the Fed maintaining its cautious stance and wanting to closely evaluate macro indicators before turning more dovish, the global money supply has grown in the past few months aided by favorable policy decisions by other central banks. Many central banks have cut rates in recent months. This list includes almost all GCC nations, Turkey, Russia, India, Mexico, Thailand, Switzerland, Australia, and even Poland. As the above chart illustrates, these expansionary policy decisions have led to a surge in M2 supply to almost $119 trillion as of mid-February. This represents YoY growth of 10.84%. This is a very interesting data point. Historically, the most aggressive BTC bull runs have coincided with strong spikes in M2 supply. More often than not, double-digit YoY growth in M2 supply has triggered Bitcoin bull runs.

Today, given geopolitical tensions, we have every reason to believe that many countries will be forced to print money to fund their military budgets. If America’s active involvement in Middle East tensions lasts more than a few days as initially expected, chances are that the U.S. will have to boost its money supply. This is consistent with historical evidence. For example, during World War II, the debt-to-GDP ratio rose to over 100% as the U.S. government was forced to print money to support its military spending.

Exhibit 2: U.S. debt-to-GDP ratio

Given the positive correlation between global M2 supply and BTC prices, I believe we are nearing an inflection point for Bitcoin.

Institutional Flows Remain Strong

Relying on a single macro indicator is not a foolproof strategy to predict a reversal of BTC prices. Over the past 12 months, institutional investors have taken a real interest in Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies. Monitoring institutional fund flows, therefore, can be an effective tool to identify trend reversals. Amid the crypto winter, Bitcoin ETFs have seen strong selling pressure since last October. According to Bloomberg data, between October 2025 and late February, Bitcoin ETFs recorded $9 billion in outflows. However, things have turned around since February 24. Since then, Bitcoin ETFs have recorded net inflows of $1.7 billion. This is a major turn of fund flows. I believe these inflows are driven by institutional investors wanting to gain exposure to Bitcoin as a hedging instrument amid global uncertainties.

Bitcoin Halving Impact May Finally Be Felt In Coming Months

The most recent Bitcoin halving occurred in April 2024 when the daily reward dropped from 900 to 450 BTC. Under normal circumstances, this should result in a lower supply of BTC. However, empirical evidence suggests that the opposite happens in the initial stages following a halving event. This is because thinly profitable miners resort to aggressive BTC divestitures to cover the cost of their mining operations. This creates selling pressure in the market. Now that we are closing in on the second anniversary of the latest halving event, I believe this treasury exhaustion among marginally profitable miners is coming to an end. This should pave the way for a supply deficit in BTC moving forward.

There is a very real possibility of this expected supply deficit meeting a strong uptick in demand amid geopolitical tensions. This could set up a perfect platform for BTC prices to break through to the upside.

Why BitFuFu Is A Great Pick To Gain Exposure To A Potential Bitcoin Comeback

In addition to investing directly in BTC, investors can invest in crypto stocks to gain exposure to a potential reversal in Bitcoin prices. As I revealed in a separate analysis a few weeks ago, I doubled down on my Coinbase long position as I believe Coinbase will benefit no matter which cryptocurrency dominates the world in the long run. My bet on Coinbase is a bet on the success of the blockchain technology.

When it comes to Bitcoin, FUFU offers a unique value proposition to long-term investors because of a few reasons.

BitFuFu is exposed to BTC prices directly through its self-mining business, but also provides a shield against fully relying on price movements with its diversification into the cloud mining business. For those who are not familiar with BitFuFu, the company’s cloud mining business, which is nestled under the mining services segment, serves 648,000 registered users who pay fees to access its services.

Exhibit 3: BitFuFu’s business model

If you take a look at some of the biggest public crypto miners, a big challenge faced by them is their reliance on block rewards. While BitFuFu also earns a chunk of its revenue from block rewards, the cloud mining business helps the company earn fee-based revenue by selling mining contracts. This makes BitFuFu one of the most diversified public crypto miners. In the third quarter of 2025, this segment accounted for ~68% of total revenue ($122.9 million).

Exhibit 4: BitFuFu’s segment revenue in Q3 2025

Despite operating in two main business segments, BitFuFu has the flexibility to allocate resources to the most rewarding business at a given point in time. This is achieved with the use of its Aladdin system which routes hashrate to maximize yield. This flexibility makes BitFuFu even more attractive as it enables the company to prioritize profitable growth rather than diversifying for the sake of it. When crypto markets turn a corner, the company can effectively prioritize the self-mining business, which exposes investors to asymmetric upside. When the opposite is true, BitFuFu can focus on its cloud mining business to generate sufficient cash to function without having to deplete its crypto treasury.

This balanced business model is one of the main reasons why I fell in love with the company a few months ago.

Energy Investments Boost BitFuFu’s Appeal

I have discussed BitFuFu’s energy investments in detail in previous analyses. I thought of touching on them yet again today given the uncertain geopolitical environment we have found ourselves in. Energy prices have already spiked due to Middle East tensions, and this is not good news for Bitcoin miners as energy is one of the biggest operating costs for every miner. Before this escalation in tensions, I always looked at BitFuFu’s energy investments as a cost-saving measure. But today, I consider these investments to provide the company with a competitive edge amid global uncertainties.

The company’s energy independence strategy is centered on expanding into regions, sometimes even remote, with cheap access to renewable energy. The Ethiopian Hydro Shield is a classic example of this. BitFuFu now operates an 80 MW data center in Ethiopia. Since this facility is powered by hydroelectricity, it makes the company less immune to oil price shocks as well.

Exhibit 5: Data center expansion

In addition to helping BitFuFu secure energy independence, these investments will also be accretive to margins given that the company is exposed to energy prices well below the world average in most of these regions.

The Healthy Balance Sheet Adds Another Layer Of Safety

Given the many uncertainties facing the crypto industry, it is almost impossible to talk about a miner’s prospects without commenting on its balance sheet health. BitFuFu, thankfully, has a very strong liquidity position today. This makes the company immune to geopolitical shocks in the foreseeable future. The company ended Q3 2025 with ~$255 million in cash and equivalents (this includes digital assets as well).

BitFuFu, in fact, ended Q3 with a negative net debt position. Against total long-term debt of $141.3 million (including payables), the company had total liquidity of $254.8 million. This translates to a net cash position of almost $114 million.

Based on BitFuFu’s January performance update, the company held 1,796 BTC on its balance sheet, up from 1,780 the month before. This MoM growth is a sign that BitFuFu is continuing to hoard BTC rather than being forced to liquidate its reserves. This is a good sign at a time when many less-profitable miners are liquidating their BTC reserves to cover operating costs.

In addition to this already strong liquidity position, BitFuFu has an option to tap into capital markets easily as well. The $150 million ATM equity offering that was approved last June still has $144 million in remaining capacity.

Risks

BitFuFu, similar to its crypto mining peers, relies heavily on favorable crypto market conditions. Despite its diversified business model, the company’s fortunes remain closely tied to BTC prices. A crypto winter that lasts years, not months, will prove to be a massive drag on its financial performance.

Investors should also keep an eye on changing energy regulations in global regions where BitFuFu has expanded into. This risk became evident when the company revealed that a new tariff structure was announced in Ethiopia late last year. While the new prices are still very economical compared to many other global regions, this highlights the need to keep a close eye on the regulatory environment for BitFuFu’s data centers.

Takeaway

Bitcoin has made a strong comeback since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran last Saturday. This is consistent with the hedging benefits associated with BTC. Looking at the long term, I believe Bitcoin prices are likely to be driven by global money supply movements rather than a risk-off trade. Based on recent M2 supply trends, BTC seems to be entering an inflection point that could help prices break through to the upside. Amid these interesting developments, I view BitFuFu offers investors a unique value proposition with its balanced business strategy and energy independence.

data.title: Broadcom: The Most Important Non-GPU AI Compounder Is Becoming Indispensable
data.description:
Not only has Broadcom Inc.’s (AVGO) impressive fiscal Q1 earnings results cleared another high bar, but they also reinforced visibility into the company’s expanding share of longer-term AI infrastructure spending. Specifically, the company grew revenue by 29% y/y to $19.3 billion during fiscal Q1. AI semiconductor sales were the bright spot, expanding 106% y/y to $8.4 billion during fiscal Q1, and representing the key driver of the semiconductor solutions segment’s 52% y/y revenue growth towards $12.5 billion during the quarter.

Momentum was further reinforced by management’s confident outlook on AI semiconductor sales, which is not only expected to accelerate by 140% y/y towards $10.7 billion in fiscal Q2, but also top $100 billion by the end of FY 2027. This trajectory implies sustained acceleration, and anchors confidence that Broadcom remains well positioned to capitalize on emerging AI bottlenecks, in particular, making it the most important non-GPU AI company in the market.

Specifically, the accelerating AI infrastructure has been hitting a few walls as of late. In addition to ongoing merchant GPU constraints, as evidenced in Nvidia’s (NVDA) recent appeal to TSMC (TSM) for additional wafer supply to address insatiable end-market demand, industry is also challenged by intensifying memory and power capacity constraints. This has effectively encouraged diversification to non-GPU compute, which is especially evident in accelerating custom XPU deployments observed across hyperscalers. Tightening memory supply and limited power budgets have also been driving demand for low latency, high bandwidth networking solutions to scale next-generation AI clusters.

Both demand shifts stem from intensifying AI infrastructure bottlenecks that are favoring Broadcom’s roadmap in custom AI XPUs and networking. The evolving dynamic also reinforces Broadcom’s share gains as AI capex starts to diversify beyond costly GPUs that’ve largely been a concentrated benefit to Nvidia in recent years.

Although Broadcom stock currently trades at a slight premium of 30x estimated earnings and 14x estimated sales relative to comparable semiconductor peers like Marvell (MRVL) and even Nvidia, durability remains. The company’s latest $100 billion AI revenue outlook for FY 2027 remains well positioned to surprise further to the upside, given Broadcom’s multi-pronged reach into burgeoning non-GPU opportunities tied to the ongoing AI infrastructure buildout.

Growing AI XPU Pipeline

Broadcom’s rooted leadership in custom AI processors (“XPUs”) is evident in its expanding pipeline, which currently consists of five primary customers plus a new addition – OpenAI (OPENAI) – announced in October.

The five existing programs – led by its anchor partnership with Google (GOOG / GOOGL) – continues to ramp well based on management’s latest update.

As I’ve discussed in a recent coverage on Google, its latest v7 “Ironwood” TPU continues to gain robust traction, thanks to the strong reception on its latest Gemini 3 model, which was trained on the in-house silicon. This momentum was further reinforced by Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) recent commitment to an additional “1 million TPUs worth tens of billions of dollars,” starting with 1GW of deployment this year and “surge in excess of 3GW” next year based on Broadcom’s recent disclosure. Anthropic’s incremental TPU uptake was followed by Apple’s (AAPL) recent partnership with Google to support next-generation “Apple Intelligence” developments, which will entail access to Gemini models and other Google cloud computing technologies. This further strengthens the case for incremental TPU adoption, reinforcing demand acceleration for current and next-generation programs that could ultimately benefit Broadcom as well.

Broadcom is also a key partner to Meta Platform’s (META) proprietary “Meta Training and Inference Accelerator” (“MTIA”) program, which the social media giant is looking to double-down on. Based on Broadcom’s latest update, the company is currently ramping MTIA shipments to Meta, and is positioned to scale next-generation XPUs to “multiple gigawatts in ’27 and beyond.” This momentum is corroborated by Meta CFO Susan Li’s latest commentary regarding the company’s plans to double-down on custom silicon, despite having recently forged several high-volume merchant GPU expansion deals with Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). Specifically, Li noted that custom silicon is a “big part” of optimizing efficiency in training some of Meta’s most curated workloads:

We are really focused on basically making sure that we are getting the optimal chip for each workload...and that combination is still at a scale that like lets us do this in a sort of cost-efficient way, if that makes sense...Custom silicon is a big part of that, obviously. Some of our workloads really are very customized to us. The sort of ranking and recommendations workloads have been where we have started, and that's the place where we have rolled out custom silicon at the most scale. But we expect and are hopeful that we are going to expand that over time, including eventually to training AI models.

Source: Meta CFO Susan Li, Morgan Stanley TMT Conference 2026.

Long-term visibility into Broadcom’s XPU pipeline is further reinforced by its recent deal win with OpenAI, with deployment of the partnership’s first custom processor expected to start next year at more than 1GW of capacity. This is further complemented by strong shipments at Broadcom’s remaining XPU portfolio, which is expected to more than double shipments in 2027.

Two-Pronged AI Networking Roadmap

In addition to custom XPUs, Broadcom’s networking leadership is also seeing additive momentum as large-scale AI clusters face deployment bottlenecks. I’ve recently discussed extensively in my analysis on Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI) and Marvell the current networking bottlenecks as AI cluster deployments scale from tens of thousands of GPUs towards millions today. Specifically, stringing together clusters tens of thousands up to millions of accelerators today requires massive scale-up (i.e., linking accelerators together within a server) and scale-out (i.e., cross-rack and cross-datacenter interconnect) networking technology to ensure low latency and high-bandwidth, optimizing compute uptime and minimizing costly idle capacity. Coupled with intensifying power constraints, sufficient networking capability becomes increasingly important in driving performance per watt and essentially optimizing token unit economics that underpin AI investment returns.

And related demand is reflected in recent momentum in Broadcom’s networking business. Specifically, the company’s AI networking revenues currently account for a third of its total fiscal Q1 AI revenue – or about $3 billion. This number is expected to accelerate by more than 50% sequentially, as management expects networking revenue contributions to grow to 40% of anticipated AI revenue (~$10.7 billion) in the current period. This strength is supported by Broadcom’s market leadership reinforced via a two-pronged approach in AI networking:

1. Direct Attach Copper (“DAC”)

Copper has long been the default interconnect in scaling communication between chips and servers using electrical signals. However, as large-scale AI deployments expand across servers and even data centers, copper’s inherent short-reach and heat distribution constraints have emerged as limitations in accelerated data centers.

For now, copper remains mostly relevant in scale-up networking only (i.e., interconnects between chips and short-reach servers/racks). This was recently reaffirmed by Broadcom management, citing the continued advantage of DAC in scale-up, as its compatibility with existing infrastructure represents a low cost, low latency option to increase time-to-deployment and generate optimal returns.

...you really want to connect XPUs to XPUs directly where you can. And the best way to do that is to use direct attach copper. That's the lowest latency, lowest power, and lowest cost. So you want to keep doing that, especially in scale-up, as long as possible.

Source: Broadcom Q1 FY 2026 Earnings Call Transcript.

This accordingly highlights Broadcom’s DAC portfolio, which currently includes switches (e.g., Tomahawk 6; 800G AI Ethernet NIC; Jericho 4) with passive DAC support with particular relevance in short-reach scale-up applications. The company also offers 800G AEC retimer solutions used in copper interconnects. As I’ve recently discussed in a Marvell analysis, active electrical cable (“AEC”) is an enhanced copper interconnect solution. Recall that traditional DAC connects are typically short-reach and prone to signal degradation in high-speed communication typically observed in accelerated AI data centers today. AEC retimers address these roadblocks by incorporating active electronics on each attaching end of the copper connect to help refine and clean up signals, effectively bypassing inherent bottlenecks in traditional DAC. The AEC solution can extend DAC connectivity beyond 7 meters, making it relevant in scale-up and short-reach scale-out use cases where the transition to optical fabrics may prove too powerful and expensive.

2. Optical/Photonics

Meanwhile, co-packaged optics is becoming increasingly critical in supporting cross-rack and cross-datacenter scale-out, as the technology addresses inherent latency, bandwidth and signal integrity bottlenecks in traditional copper interconnects. This is corroborated by Broadcom management’s recent acknowledgement that the transition to optical has become mission critical in the scale-out of accelerated data centers:

In scaling out, we're past that. We use optical.

Source: Broadcom Q1 FY 2026 Earnings Call Transcript.

As I’ve discussed in detail in a recent coverage, optical AI interconnect fabrics have become increasingly relevant in scaling large accelerator cluster deployments. These networking structures rely on fiber and optical transceivers to ferry data communication signals between servers and switches across longer-reach racks and data centers. The servers and switches are linked by fiber with an optical transceiver on each end. Data signals are beamed in the form of light through the fiber, which can support longer-reach links, higher bandwidth, lower latency and better signal integrity. Meanwhile, the optical transceivers on each end of the fiber interconnect handles the conversion between the electrical domain (i.e., signals inside the chips, servers, and switches) and the optical domain (i.e., signals carried over the connecting fiber lanes), processing the receipt and transmission of data signal communications between servers.

Broadcom is currently the leader in CPO-based switches. Its CPO roadmap currently supports optical/photonics networking fabrics by integrating silicon photonics solutions directly with Ethernet switch ASICs and XPUs. This further relieves networking bottlenecks in scaling out large AI cluster deployments today by enabling even higher bandwidth density, reduced consumption per bit, and lower latency compared to pluggable optics where the conversion between the electric and optical domains occur outside the switch/server.

Fundamental Considerations

Taken together, Broadcom’s current pipeline and product roadmap reinforces its trajectory towards at least $100 billion AI revenue by FY 2027. This implies sustained acceleration in AI semiconductor sales at more than 100% over the next two years at least, supported by robust visibility into Broadcom’s custom XPU pipeline, which subsequently reinforces adoption of its networking portfolio for scaling cluster deployments as well.

Based on the foregoing analysis, my forecast projects revenue growth of 50% y/y to $96.1 billion in FY 2026, with further acceleration at 68% y/y towards $161.1 billion in FY 2027.

The high-visibility growth trajectory will also maintain Broadcom’s durability. This is corroborated by management’s guidance for sustained gross margin at 77% and adjusted EBITDA at 68%, despite an increasing mix shift towards less profitable semiconductor solution sales. Continued acceleration in AI revenue share gains will remain a key driver of sustained operating leverage improvement and FCF accretion long-term.

Price Consideration

Based on the above fundamental projections, I’m setting a base case price target of $353 per share for Broadcom stock.

The price target is derived under the discounted cash flow (“DCF”) approach, which analyses cash flow projections based on the fundamental forecast above. A terminal value of $2.1 trillion is considered, which is determined by the application of a 3.5% perpetual growth rate on projected FY 2030 EBITDA. The 3.5% perpetual growth rate imitates the higher-end of anticipated long-term economic expansion across Broadcom’s key markets. This assumption reflects the company’s sustainable cash flow accretion runway at steady-state (proxied by terminal EBITDA), as its high-growth AI opportunities scale over time. Total cash flow projections, including the terminal value, are discounted at a 9.7% WACC, which aligns with Broadcom’s capital structure and risk profile.

Risks to Consider

Admittedly, ongoing supply chain constraints remain the key overhang on AI chipmakers. This is reflected in Nvidia’s recent acknowledgement of sustained wafer supply insufficiency at TSMC – which is also Broadcom’s key manufacturing partner. The wafer supply constraint was further corroborated by commentary by Apple during its latest earnings update. Specifically, Apple cited limitations in advanced process node capacity – particularly the 3nm node – that its SoCs for the iPhone are produced on. This is the same process node on which many of Broadcom’s best-sellers, including the Tomahawk 6 and Jericho 4, relies on, in addition to its XPU designs.

Although Broadcom has expressed confidence that it’s secured supplies to see through the robust revenue outlook through FY 2027, potential for better-than-expected demand – as evidenced by its roadmap’s increasing relevance in scaling next-generation deployments – could become restrained. This could potentially limit upside from current levels.

Today, in fact, we have line of sight to achieve AI revenue from chips, just chips, in excess of $100 billion in 2027. We have also secured the supply chain required to achieve this...We anticipate this sharp accelerated growth, now nobody could anticipate the rate of growth is showing, but we kind of anticipate a large part of it, I guess, for longer than 6 months. We were early in being able to lock up T-glass. It's the infamous T-glass you all heard about. We were very early. We've locked up substrates. We have worked on our good partners on the rest of the stuff we talked about. And so the answer to your question is, it's somewhat anticipation early and the fact that we have very good partners out there in these key components.

Source: Broadcom Q1 FY 2026 Earnings Call Transcript.

Final Thoughts

Broadcom’s latest earnings update has reinforced visibility into its long-term growth and earnings profile. In addition to its tone of support for sustained AI infrastructure demand, Broadcom’s favorable outlook has also strengthened the case that it’s emerging as a key beneficiary as spending priorities shift. Specifically, it’s emerging as a key share gainer in the next phase of AI capex that’s rapidly diversifying beyond merchant GPUs and towards custom XPUs and low-latency networking to address impending scale bottlenecks. This accordingly makes Broadcom one of the clearest non-GPU compounders, with the stock exhibiting strong upside potential from current levels supported by tangible fundamentals.

data.title: Weather And Strikes Weigh On February U.S. Jobs
data.description:
By James Knightley, Chief International Economist, US

Jobs shrink, unemployment rises, spending falls

US non-Farm payrolls fell 92k in February versus the +55k consensus and there were a net 69k downward revisions to the previous two months (January 2026 and December 2025). The unemployment rate has risen to 4.4% from 4.3%, while January retail sales fell 0.2% month-on-month versus the 0.3% drop that was expected. This is clearly not a good set of numbers, but the weather almost certainly played a significant role here.

The week the data for January was collected was relatively warm in what was a very cold month, and that helped boost construction numbers as sites remained open (+48k). In contrast, February was filled with winter storms, impacting construction the other way and hurting leisure and hospitality as people stayed in the warm at home. There was also strike activity, and that contributed to physicians’ offices subtracting 37k from the total. The details show construction falling 11k, manufacturing down 12k, leisure and hospitality down 27k. Conversely, financial services rose 10k, and retail jobs increased 2k.

The +126k payrolls number for January probably overstated the strength in the jobs market, and today's February data probably overstates the weakness due to the weather and strikes. Nonetheless, we know hiring is weak, but lay-offs remain low too. Essentially, the jobs market is just treading water, with payrolls trending at around 50k per month. The job concentration risk point still holds, though. Three sectors (government, leisure & hospitality and private education & healthcare services) are still responsible for all job creation over the past three years. All other sectors combined (essentially the bulk of the US economy) have lost 460k jobs since December 2022.

Rate cuts delayed on near-term inflation

We know that there are more unemployed Americans than there are job vacancies, and this is now depressing wage growth. Average earnings did rise 3.8% year-on-year in today’s report, but that is likely due to lower-wage workers not being able to get to work and not being reported and skewing the reported earnings in favour of higher-earning office workers who could work from home. The broader measure of wage growth within the employment cost index risks dropping below 3% YoY and we already know that real household disposable incomes are flatlining. The surge in energy costs, particularly for gasoline, that we expect over the next few weeks in response to global market moves, means that we could see real disposable incomes turning negative. That risks creating an intensifying headwind for growth in the second half of the year.

While higher energy costs are inflationary and make near-term rate cuts look less probable, it also puts more pressure on consumer finances and can ultimately be demand-destructive. This can push core inflation pressures lower over the medium to longer term. Therefore, we argue the story is one where rate cuts are delayed rather than removed from forecasts. We have pushed our Fed rate cut forecasts from June and September to September and December.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: UFP Industries: Limited Growth Outlook And Elevated Valuation Justify Hold
data.description:
Investment Thesis

UFP Industries, Inc. (UFPI) continues to face challenges in the Construction segment due to weak residential demand. However, the Retail segment is expected to provide support to the revenue with strong demand for proprietary products. Moreover, the Packaging segment showed signs of recovery in the latest quarter. On the profitability front, the cost-cutting program and improved capacity utilization should be accretive. Moreover, the company is trading at a premium of ~42% to its 5-year average P/E. Given the limited revenue growth outlook and valuation premium, I recommend a Hold rating on the stock.

Prior Coverage Summary

I covered UFP Industries on May 16, 2025, and I assigned a Hold rating on the stock due to weak demand in most of its segments and elevated valuation. At that time, my major concerns were softness in construction activity and weak demand in the Packaging segment. Moreover, I also expected the pressure on margins from competitive pricing and higher input costs. Since my article was published, the total return earned by the stock was -2.6%; however, the S&P 500 (market index) has gained about 15%. Hence, the stocks have underperformed the overall market since my coverage in 2025. This follow-up analysis examines the company's future outlook after the latest earnings release and evaluates whether recent factors across the segments change the original investment thesis.

Business Overview

UFP Industries is the producer of products that are made from wood, non-wood composites, and other materials. The company has three business segments: Retail, Packaging, and Construction segment. Through the Retail segment, the company sells lumber and mineral-based products, including decking, fencing, and other related accessories. The Packaging segment manufacturers structural packaging products made from wood, metal, foam, labels, and strapping. The Construction segment deals in roof trusses, plywood, and I-joints for factory-built houses and residential & light commercial projects.

Revenue Analysis & Outlook

In the fourth quarter of FY2025, the company reported $1.33 billion in net sales, a decrease of 9% year-over-year due to a decline in sales volume. While it is true that the growth in Deckorators business of the Retail segment continued, the Construction segment faced headwinds from lower residential demand. Moreover, in the quarter, the Packaging segment showed some early signals of stabilization.

Looking ahead, I believe the revenue of the company should be broadly flat year-over-year in FY2026, supported by the growth in the less cyclical Retail segment. Moreover, the Packaging segment shows some signs of recovery. However, the Construction segment should continue to face weakness from macroeconomic uncertainty in the coming quarters.

The Retail segment of UFPI is linked to the Repair & Remodelling industry in the U.S. The overall market should witness weakened growth momentum in FY2026. Leading Indicator of Remodelling Activity (LIRA) forecasts that the year-over-year growth rate in home repair should decrease from 2.9% in the first half of FY2026 to the 1.6% in the end of FY26. While key indicators suggest weakness, the sales from the Retail segment are expected to improve in FY2026. This should be driven by market share gains and increased capacity in the Deckorators business. UFPI's management anticipates approximately $100 million in incremental sales from Deckorators business, driven by strong demand for proprietary Surestone products and improved production at Selma & Buffalo. Both production plants are progressing towards increased capacity, which should be completed by the first quarter of FY26. Given increased production, along with market share gain, the company should continue to drive improved sales in the coming quarters.

The Packaging segment should normalize in the coming quarters of FY2026. The US Manufacturing Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) is a leading indicator for the company's Packaging segment as with higher manufacturing activity, the demand for structural packaging (used in logistics) increases. PMI in February stood at 51.6 above the 50.0 threshold, indicating expansion in the manufacturing activity. However, the recent PMI was down from previous month by 80 points, due to the high prices, tariffs and bad weather conditions. The future outlook for the segment is limited right now, due to the tariff uncertainty and lumber price volatility. Nevertheless, The volume of Structural Packaging increased by 1% year-over-year, which is the first positive y-o-y comparison since FY2021. The management believes that the segment should witness better than market growth once industrial demand recovers through strategic new customer wins. But the near-term performance should be dependent on macroeconomic conditions and recovery in the manufacturing activity.

The performance in the Construction segment should remain weak in FY2026, due to lower demand in the housing market. The challenges in the housing market include affordability issues resulting from elevated mortgage rates. The policy rate set by the Federal Reserve affects the mortgage rate. Even with recent rate cuts, the federal funds rate remains above the Fed's long-run neutral estimate of 3.0%. The management of UFP believes that the first half of FY2026 should remain challenging due to persistent volume weakness. While it is true that Factor-Built and commercial businesses should provide some support to the revenue, the overall growth of the segment depends on the recovery of the residential market.

Margin Analysis & Outlook

In the fourth quarter of FY2025, the adjusted EBITDA margin stood at 8.1%, a decrease of 100 basis points year-over-year. This decrease was due to lower operating leverage from volume decline. Moreover, the net income margin also fell to 3% from 4.8%, implying a decline of 180 basis points from the previous year’s quarter.

Looking ahead, I believe the margins should improve in FY2025, supported by benefits from the cost-out program and capacity optimization. While the Construction segment should be a headwind in the near-term, the disciplined cost management and production efficiency should drive the margin expansion in the coming quarters.

At the beginning of 2025, the management set a goal to achieve $60 million in cost reductions by the end of FY2026. This is expected to be completed with half of the cost reductions from the Selling, General & Administration (SG&A) and the remaining from the consolidation of capacity, resulting in reduced cost of goods sold. Given the cost reduction program, the margin is expected to expand in the coming quarters.

The margins of the Retail segment should improve in FY2026, primarily driven by the proper utilization of Deckorators' capacity, which was underutilized in 2025. The company estimates incremental sales from the Deckorators business, which should support margin expansion through operating leverage and improved capacity utilization. While the marketing spend should remain elevated, the improved production should support the overall margin expansion.

The margins in the Construction segment should remain under pressure due to the softness in the residential market. Although the cost controls should limit the negative impacts on the margins, the limited volume leverage should constrain the margin recovery in the near future.

The Packaging segment’s margin should stabilize in FY26 with improvements from volume normalization. Moreover, the automation investment and operational efficiency should be accretive to the margins. While these factors should support the margins, the overall expansion is likely to be dependent on the recovery of industrial demand in the future.

Valuation

UFP Industries is currently trading at a non-GAAP P/E (fwd) of 18.19x with a FY2026 consensus EPS estimate of $5.32. The revenue of the company should be supported by the less cyclical repair and remodel business, including the strong demand in the Deckorators business. Moreover, the packaging segment also shows signs of recovery with good potential when industrial demand improves. However, the construction segment is expected to continue to face near-term challenges from affordability issues. The margins should be supported by the improved capacity utilization and cost-cutting program. Given these factors, I believe the company should achieve the consensus estimate. Furthermore, I have used relative valuation to compare the company along with its peer on the basis of revenue growth rate, operating margin, and P/E ratios. Looking at the revenue growth outlook, UFP Industries is expected to decline by 0.62%, which is lower than the peer forward revenue growth rate of about 2.33%. Moreover, on the profitability front, the operating margin (TTM) of the company stood at 5.77%, which is significantly lower than the peer median margin of approximately 11.9%. Comparing UFPI's P/E ratio of 18.19 with its peers, it is below the median P/E ratio. This discount seems justified to me, given a weaker outlook and a lower margin profile.

Risk

My investment thesis is premised on the momentum in the Retail segment, with the strong demand in Deckorators business. Moreover, the Packaging segment is expected to deliver results from the recovery of industrial demand. However, if the macroeconomic conditions worsen, it may further negatively impact the overall company’s performance. Additionally, if mortgage rates continue to be higher than expected, it may further impact housing affordability and could negatively impact the results from the Construction segment.

Conclusion

With a P/E ratio of 18.19x and a 5-year average P/E ratio of 13.19x, the company is trading at a premium of approximately 40%. However, when compared to the sector median of ~20.9x, the company is at a discount of ~10%. UFP Industries’ potential to beat the consensus estimates depends on the macroeconomic conditions and the recovery in demand from its end markets. While the company is at a discount to its peers, I believe the entry point is not adequate to justify the risk/reward ratio due to the macroeconomic uncertainty looming over the company's exposure to cyclical end markets. Given these factors and a premium to the 5-year average P/E ratio, there should be a Hold rating on the stock.

data.title: Total Energies: Then Came Iran
data.description:
The last article mentioned buying Total Energies (TTE) in preference to Repsol (REPYY) due to its superior profitability. Before that came an article on the high debt rating of Total and its push to get the highest (AA) debt rating in the industry for a super major. The Iran crisis (or challenges) is going to show the advantage of a super major with a darn good balance sheet in a crisis situation that is characteristic of this very volatile and low visibility industry.

We still do not know how long the Iran crisis will last as it affects the oil industry. Many reports have the Strait of Hormuz closed. If it stays closed for a length of time it could very much alter how the industry does business while raising some commodity prices in the process. Many of these crises that happen resolve themselves rather quickly as it affects the industry (even if the politics take longer).

But right now, the best thing to do with many ideas is to be patient. The oil industry as a whole has runup considerably from before the crisis. There is every chance that the runup will backtrack either from a resolution to the current situation (quickly) as it affects the industry or possibly from a loss of interest due to something else that attracts our interest with our notoriously short attention span.

A super major like Total is likely able to shift resources as the industry accommodates the situation to maximize profits. Just about any oil company learns to navigate the politics where it operates. Oil and gas by its nature is very much involved in all kinds of political situations. So, navigating the Iran situation as it unfolds is not really new territory in a sense. Total is in a better position than many smaller competitors due to its extensive geographic diversification. That makes it far easier to shift resources to areas that benefit (if needed) while "starving" areas that are not going to do well.

Southern Africa

One of the areas that just may benefit from the closed Straits of Hormuz may be the West Coast of Africa all the way down to South Africa. Total has locations on the West Coast in several places.

Some of the significant areas that may benefit from the current situation are:

Depending upon what happens with the Iranian situation, the most likely project to potentially benefit from the whole thing is the Venus project in Namibia. An FID is very likely in fiscal year 2026 with production a year or so later. An extended Iranian situation that leads to a long-term closure of the Strait of Hormuz is likely to very much benefit that Venus project where production does not have to go anywhere near those Straits no matter which direction that production goes. The European market is large enough to be a long-term attraction for that production.

Remember that there are other possible solutions for various markets if the Straits of Hormuz remains closed for "years".

The key is that a company like this is able to speed up development of resources if there is a critical need because a critical need often means a faster payback (and hence a very profitable start to the project production).

Meanwhile, if it is hard to get production to market elsewhere, then that production will just decline until the situation changes. The hard work is getting started in the first place in the right locations. This company has so many locations that some of them are bound to be "right" and likely outweigh any challenges.

Earnings

When it comes to earnings, a whole lot of things "went out the window". A simple example is the conference call on the website for Ring Energy (REI). There, management mentioned how they were busy hedging for what they thought was a year of weaker prices ahead (so hedge now to get decent pricing all year). But then along came Iran and that playbook went "out the window". Now it is more about riding price up and then trying to figure out what to do in this very volatile and low visibility industry. The problem right now is that just about any idea could be right. It is extremely hard to tell what to wait for when it comes to future earnings and a solid strategy.

Last year's earnings and really budgets in general are basically "on hold" until the industry can get a handle on how to plan the near future.

In such a situation, I like a company like this because it often has a location advantage that outweighs any other issues (which are inevitable when a company is located "everywhere"). Companies like this often come out of a relatively fluid situation (as is currently the case) much better than those with lower rated debt.

Summary

When there is a crisis like the Iranian crisis, I very much like to be invested in larger companies like Total Energies because a company like this will generally find a way to make money in a way that outperforms much of the industry.

Yes, the company is located throughout the world, so there will be some locations that are at a disadvantage. But rearranging resources to make favored locations more significant than disadvantaged locations is one of the easiest things to do in this industry. The hard part is being there in the first place (and the initial startup).

For me, Total Energies remains a strong buy consideration. However, I would first be patient enough to be able to determine if the Iranian crisis (as it affects the industry) will be a long-term or a short-term time situation. Once that is determined, then I will know whether I want to chase the stock price higher or wait for the usual drop back to the "before crisis price". Many times, these challenges pass very quickly and normal business can resume. Mr. Market heads to the next crisis very quickly. Patience can be worth a lot of money in this industry.

While the current price runup may be a trading situation, the situation requires someone with a lot of time to monitor "everything" to maximize the profit potential of the trade (or at least make a decent profit). It is not a situation for the average investor that has to work or gets busy with family and kids.

Once I am invested, I would consider holding this stock until the current story changes materially for the worse.

Risks

The biggest risk right now is any challenges regarding the Iranian crisis. Figuring out how long the challenges will last and what their effect will be is no easy task. It is very easy to go wrong and require one or more course corrections until the situation heads back to normal.

Commodity prices even in the best of times are both volatile and low visibility. A severe and sustained downturn can materially change the company outlook.

The management that has improved this company tremendously over the last 10 years is a subset of all the managers eligible for senior management positions. Therefore, the loss of services of a key person could well set back the future of the company. That person lost for any reason could prove to be a real challenge to replace.

data.title: Broadcom: Making A Solid Case For Its Nvidia Moment
data.description:
Broadcom Is Enjoying The Limelight Now

Is it finally time for Broadcom Inc.’s (AVGO) to replicate Nvidia’s (NVDA) finest moment? Well, the fact that Nvidia stock has been trending sideways despite posting what I thought was a massive beat and raise earnings scorecard recently, I believe, suggests that the market was still looking for more juice, from the King of AI. After becoming the world's most valuable company, Nvidia finished fiscal 2026 with almost 66% in revenue growth, a number that is arguably an envy even for companies that are half its market cap.

Because, if that's not considered spectacular, I really don't know what else you are looking for. But we have to respect the market’s wisdom, right? There must be something the market knows, as it has reallocated to NVDA’s peers such as Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and even Broadcom. While Nvidia remains the king of AI and appears far from being dethroned, that doesn't mean that its archrivals couldn't mount a more sustained campaign to gain share in the next few years.

I believe that is the exact narrative that Broadcom CEO Hock Tan wants us to take away from the company's recent earnings conference. It is pretty clear that management has attempted to provide much confidence that the growth momentum is expected to benefit not only the custom AI chips business, or the one more commonly known as XPUs, but also networking, which has typically been Broadcom’s bread and butter, so to speak. And guess what, even AMD management wanted to paint a scenario that they are getting into the custom business, but not amounting to an ASIC chip that is arguably the domain expertise of Broadcom, as management keenly reminds us:

We've been doing this for 20 years, more than 20 years in silicon. And in this particular space today in generative AI, if you're trying to, as an LLM player, to do your own chip, you cannot afford to have a chip that is just good enough. You need the best chips that is around because you're competing against other LLM players. And most of all, you're also competing against NVIDIA, who is by no means letting down their guard. They are producing better and better chips with every passing generation. So you have to -- as an LLM trying to establish your platform in the world, have to create chips that are better than, if not competitive with, not just NVIDIA, but all the other platform players that you're competing against. - Broadcom Inc. Q1 FY2026 Earnings Call.

Broadcom's Custom AI Chips Programs Are Accelerating

So here you go. Broadcom provides a timely nudge to remind us that cutting edge semiconductors aren't something you can simply design and build quickly, as we saw for ourselves just how challenging it is. It isn't surprising or coincidental that Meta Platforms (META) has signed up with Google (GOOGL) to deploy TPUs, just as reports came in that it faced challenges in its own homegrown chips. Similarly, even software behemoth Microsoft (MSFT) is also trying to convince the market that it is capable of deploying its custom accelerators. Nevertheless, it is also good to see that Marvell is ramping up its XPU programs with Microsoft and reportedly Amazon (AMZN). I believe it's strongly suggests that hyperscalers are clearly positioning themselves for the era of custom XPU workloads, as compared to just focusing on merchant AI chips through Nvidia from 2026 and even 2027, which suggests that the market could be more concerned with whether NVDA can still replicate its incredible growth momentum after 2027.

If anything, it does show that Google, with TPU now in the 7th generation, and to a certain extent Amazon, with the recent Trainium 3 deployment prospects with OpenAI (OPENAI), have shown that they have the scale and chip design capabilities. But note that Amazon has also co-designed its chips, including in partnership with Marvell. It's important for us to know that while Amazon is not known to be working with Broadcom on custom accelerators, it does not invalidate Broadcom’s expertise and capabilities, especially since Amazon may not be too keen on paying the premium for Broadcom’s service. But if Google's incredible results were to show, I think there's no doubt that working with Broadcom closely over the years has arguably been one of the best decisions that Mountain View has mustered. And moving forward, programs will focus not just on combined training and inference chips, but also separate ones, with Broadcom expected to take charge of the training chip, while competitor MediaTek is slated for the TPUv8 inference chip.

And due to that, Broadcom has amassed a growing roster of custom chip customers in its order list, notably big tech customers at the hyperscale level, since these are the ones that could really afford a custom AI chip program. No surprises, then, that it reported such remarkable performance, with a record $19.3 billion in revenue for the first fiscal quarter, while posting an adjusted EBITDA margin of >68.5%.

This indicates that Broadcom is simply operating at such incredible scale, that it is just difficult for us to envisage how smaller design rivals could even dethrone it, as customers are looking for even more optimized, lower cost efficient workloads as AI capital expenditure goes through the roof. But since Meta needs to keep up its custom accelerator efforts despite striking a deal with AMD on merchant chips, this will keep Broadcom very busy as it works toward a multi gigawatt capacity cadence.

In essence, Broadcom is showing us that it has revenue visibility for its AI chips through 2028, anticipated to surpass $100 billion in AI revenue by 2027. Recall that management just provided guidance that suggests Broadcom could post AI revenue of $10.7B by FQ2, indicating the business will more than double in the next fiscal year. Therefore, I believe the triple digit growth rates are finally finding realization among investors that Broadcom could indeed see its own Nvidia moment coming into effect earlier than we had even anticipated. And this really corroborates the momentum and driving forces underpinning the incredible demand factors for custom AI programs. And since Broadcom also sells the networking systems, it could simply capture tremendous scale and integration benefits, particularly through its Ethernet scale-out, commanding tremendous operating leverage as these scale to higher yields.

With nothing much in the way expected to stymie its growth acceleration through FY2028, I guess the only significant worry isn't so much about whether Broadcom could execute, given its prowess. The lingering concerns will likely focus more on whether its supply chain could credibly deliver. During the conference, I think management has made it clear that they don't see the supply chain as a main risk, in that it has secured the necessary capacity with its partners. Therefore, I think it conveys significant visibility for Broadcom to scale to the $190B revenue mark by FY2028, while also operating at an incredible 51.9% free cash flow margin.

Unless there is a massive cancellation across the board among the hyperscalers, since custom chip programs are highly concentrated among these extremely well resourced customers, I don't expect any major hiccups that could scupper its ability to continue getting share through the XPUs and networking programs. These are multi-generational efforts, not something you could simply cancel and forget, you know what I mean.

Buy Or Sell AVGO Stock?

What is surprising is that even though Broadcom is expected to mount a sustained offensive to gain share against Nvidia, the market hasn't really gotten onto its narrative ever since the stock pulled back in late 2025. Notably, I observe that AVGO’s multiple of just under 25x, isn't that far off NVDA’s 22x. So, it suggests to me that investors are still having second thoughts right now, especially since Nvidia is preparing for spring GTC, as the market awaits what Jensen Huang has up his sleeves this time around.

However, I think the zeitgeist has clearly shifted in favor of AVGO, as demonstrated by the above chart. AVGO/NVDA has been making credible higher lows and higher highs since November 2024. And this uptrend bias has been maintained resiliently through all the recent pullbacks, suggesting that the market has grown increasingly confident in Broadcom’s execution.

While I'm not going to say Broadcom will be printing revenue similar to the scale of Nvidia anytime soon, and also, I think there's an upper limit to how much share gains you can make against merchant chips dominated by NVDA, the next two years look clear to help AVGO open up a sizeable valuation disconnect against its more illustrious peer.

Rating: Maintain buy.

data.title: Why Investors Should Consider An Emerging Markets Bonds Allocation In 2026
data.description:
EM bonds outperformed in 2025 and the case of allocating continues to be supported by a weaker US dollar and stronger EM fundamentals in 2026.

In 2025, emerging markets faced headwinds from the Trump administration’s tariff policies, which expected to weigh on EM economies, as well as consumer and corporate weakness in China that was feared would spill over across Asia.

Instead, emerging markets thrived, challenging outdated perceptions of political and economic instability.

As shown in Table 1, emerging markets bonds outperformed global developed markets bonds by 8.71% in 2025 and US bonds by 9.64%.

Table 1: EM Bonds Outperformed Global and US Bonds in 2025

Asset Class 2025 Return
Emerging Market Bonds 16.79
Global Bonds 8.08
US Bonds 7.15

Source: VanEck. Emerging market bonds are represented by the 50% J.P. Morgan Emerging Market Bond Index Global Diversified and 50% J.P. Morgan Government Bond-Emerging Market Index Global Diversified. Global Bonds is represented by the ICE BofA Global Broad Market Index. US Bonds is represented by the ICE BofA US Broad Market Index. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Index performance is not representative of fund performance. It is not possible to invest directly in an index.

Many investors missed this opportunity, but it’s not too late.

Why Emerging Markets Bonds Were Ignored, and Why That’s Changing in 2026

The fundamental case for investing in emerging markets bonds has been building for some time, but investors have shown little interest in the asset class over the past several years. Many investors view emerging markets bonds as risky. However, we believe that is changing, emerging markets bonds are now entering a favorable phase. Despite heightened geopolitical noise and renewed trade tensions, emerging markets bonds rallied in 2025.

Exhibit 1: Emerging Markets Bonds Rallied in 2025

Source: Morningstar as of 31/12/2024 to 31/12/2025. Global Broad Market is represented by the ICE BofA Global Broad Market Index. US Broad Market is represented by the ICE BofA US Broad Market Index. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Index performance is not representative of fund performance. It is not possible to invest directly in an index.

This mirrors the early stages of the 2018 trade war (between US and China), but with a key difference: the US dollar is weakening, and markets are increasingly positioning for this shift. Actual performance, rather than headlines, is driving investor outcomes.

Exhibits 2 and 3: EM Bonds Under Trump’s Tariffs in 2018 and 2025 Differ

EM Fixed Income in Early Stages of Trade War 1.0 (2018/19)

EM Fixed Income in Early Stages of Trade War 2.0 (2025/26)

Source: VanEck Research. Data as of February 2026. EM Corporate is represented by the J.P. Morgan CEMBI Broad Diversified Index which tracks the performance of US dollar-denominated bonds issued by emerging market corporate entities.; EM Local is represented by the J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Core which tracks local currency bonds issued by emerging markets governments. The index weighting methodology limits the weight of countries with larger debt stocks, with a maximum of 10% and a minimum of 1% to 3% depending on the amount of the country’s eligible debt outstanding.; EM Sovereign is represented by the J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified Index which is comprised of U.S. dollar-denominated Brady bonds, Eurobonds, and traded loans issued by emerging markets sovereign and quasi-sovereign entities. The index weighting methodology limits the weight of countries with larger debt stocks. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Index performance is not representative of fund performance. It is not possible to invest directly in an index.

US Stagflation Risk is a Structural Tailwind

One of the reasons the US dollar is depreciating is that the US currently faces rising stagflation risk. Inflation is remaining elevated relative to growth. In contrast, inflation across many emerging markets, particularly China, has been structurally lower. Historically, such inflation differentials point to currency weakness in higher-inflation economies, reinforcing the case for US dollar depreciation. Valuation measures support this view, with the US dollar appearing overvalued and the Chinese yuan undervalued on a real effective basis.

Exhibit 4: The US Dollar Appears Overvalued vs. the Chinese Yuan

USD and CNY - Real Effective Exchange Rates

Source: VanEck Research; Bloomberg LP. Data as of January 2026. Not intended as a prediction of future results. For illustrative purposes only. Past performance is not indicative of future performance.

External Balance Sheets Advantage; Compelling Yields

Net international investment position (NIIP) data show that many emerging markets countries, particularly in Asia, are net external creditors, while the US remains a large net debtor. These accumulated surpluses of emerging markets are significant and enhance fundamental quality. In addition, many accumulated surpluses may be re-shored to home or other non-US shores. This dynamic implies either a weaker US dollar or structurally higher US rates; both outcomes are supportive for emerging market bonds.

Exhibit 5: NIIP Projects a Weaker USD

Net International Investment Position (NIIP)

Source: Deutsche Bank. Data as of December 2025. Not intended as a prediction of future results. For illustrative purposes only. Past performance is not indicative of future performance.

In addition, when hedged into Asian currencies, US Treasuries now offer low or unattractive yields, while onshore Asian and emerging markets bonds remain compelling on a relative basis. Exhibit 6 shows 30-year government bond yields hedged back into Japanese yen. When Japanese (or other Asian) investors hedge US Treasuries or European government bonds back into their home currency, the hedging cost wipes out most of the yield. As a result, hedged US Treasuries deliver low returns compared with domestic bonds such as Japanese government bonds (JGBs). This highlights a structural shift: for global investors who manage currency risk, holding US Treasuries is no longer compelling, while local Asian bonds look relatively more attractive. The chart also points out that as this reality sinks in, hedging behavior is evolving, reinforcing capital flows away from USD assets and supporting non-USD bond markets, including emerging market local-currency bonds.

Exhibit 6: Yields Wiped Out by Hedging Costs

30-Year Government Bonds Hedged Back into JPY

Source: VanEck Research; Bloomberg LP. Data as of February 2026. Past performance is not indicative of future performance.

We believe an allocation to emerging markets bonds is supported by the above. It is important to take the right approach.

Why Active, Blended?

Over the past 20 years, local currency was the best performing category within emerging markets bonds for 7 years, while the US dollar (emerging markets sovereign) took the spot for 13 years. A blended approach allows for less extreme results while capturing potential outperformance.

Exhibit 7 plots the 12-month rolling return difference between emerging markets US dollar sovereign bonds (EMBIG) and emerging markets local currency bonds (GBI-EM). When the line is above zero, US dollar emerging markets bonds outperformed. When it is below zero, local currency emerging markets bonds outperformed. Over the past 20 years, leadership has rotated frequently and sharply between the two.

This variability means that allocating to only one segment requires strong and often uncertain macro calls. A blended emerging markets bond approach helps smooth outcomes across market cycles, reduces reliance on forecasting currency or rate moves, and allows investors to capture diversified sources of return from both credit and local market dynamics.

Exhibit 7: Leadership Between Local and US Dollar Denominated EM Bonds Rotate Frequently and Sharply

EMBIG-GBI EM Total Return Differential, (12m rolling, bps)

Source: VanEck Research, Bloomberg LP. Data as of December 2025. EMBIG is represented by J.P. Morgan Emerging Market Bond Index Global Diversified, GBI-EM is represented by the JPMorgan Government Bond Index-Emerging Markets Global Diversified Index. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Index performance is not representative of fund performance. It is not possible to invest directly in an index.

Ready to Allocate? We Recommend an Active Blended Approach

In addition, due to the idiosyncrasies between the countries included in the emerging markets bond universe and the nuances between the different types of bonds available, we believe an active, unconstrained, blended approach, like the one employed by the VanEck Emerging Markets Bond ETF (EMBX) is an ideal way for investors to access this important asset class.

VanEck’s unconstrained approach offers several benefits, including greater diversification versus approaches limited to only hard or local currency. We believe an optimal portfolio of EM bonds is unconstrained by indices and invests in bonds that offer the best value relative to their fundamentals while actively managing risk.

Morgan Emerging Markets Bond Index (EMBI). Global bonds are represented by the ICE BofA Global Broad Market Index. US Bonds are represented by the ICE BofA US Broad Market Index.
